articles.title: Punk Rock Egrets: Feathered Mohawks Steal the Show in Smithsonian Photo Contest
articles.summary: Three baby egrets with spiky feathers that look exactly like punk rock mohawks have won the grand prize in Smithsonian Magazine's 23rd annual photography contest. Photographer Jacqueline Burke, a 64-year-old veterinarian, captured the chicks at a rookery in Ocean City, New Jersey. She says they were "full of attitude" and squabbling nonstop. The contest drew over 17,000 submissions from 108 countries. Other winners include a barefoot woman praying with oil lamps during a Hindu festival in India, a Navajo teen's coming-of-age ceremony with her horse, a rare chameleon in South Africa, and a stunning circular building in China. The photos celebrate nature, culture, and human diversity.

articles.title: Threads of Change: How Awamaki Helps Quechua Women Weave a Future in Peru's Sacred Valley
articles.summary: In Peru's Sacred Valley, Quechua women have woven textiles for centuries, but modern life demands cash for school, medicine, and transport. Many men leave for tourism jobs, leaving women dependent on others. Enter Awamaki, a nonprofit founded in 2009 that now supports nine cooperatives of 174 women weavers. The women raise alpacas, shear, spin, and dye wool, then weave it into goods sold globally. Climate change — melting glaciers, erratic weather — threatens their farming, but Awamaki's steady income helps families adapt without leaving their communities. Weavers like Daniela and Nadia say the cooperative gives them a voice in family decisions and pride in their craft.

articles.title: Azerbaijan Demolishes Armenian Church in Artsakh, Satellite Images Confirm
articles.summary: Satellite imagery obtained by Caucasus Heritage Watch (CHW) confirms that Azerbaijan has demolished the Holy Mother of God Church in Stepanakert, the former capital of Artsakh (Nagorno-Karabakh). The church, consecrated in 2019 after 12 years of construction, served as a bomb shelter during the 2020 war. Its destruction comes just days before the 111th anniversary of the Armenian Genocide. Since the forced displacement of over 130,000 Armenians in 2023, Azerbaijan has systematically destroyed Armenian cultural sites, which scholars call "cultural genocide." The news also follows the resignation of the Armenian Genocide Museum-Institute director after a dispute with Prime Minister Pashinyan over Artsakh policy.
